system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 7: I vi ii V and iii vi ii V

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 7 — I vi ii V and iii vi ii V

_Pages 26-48._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## I vi ii V progression definition

> Another important diatonic harmonic formula is I vi ii V (and the closely related iii vi ii V). This progression has
> been used as much as probably any other in the history of music. It is especially common to find this progression used
> as an “opener”, meaning the first progression in a song or piece.

**Significance (p. 26).** Defines the chapter's central harmonic concept and its structural role as an opener.

## Fingering attention rule

> Remember to pay close attention to the left hand fingering where it is indicated.

**Significance (p. 26).** Direct instructional rule to observe notated fingerings.

## Melody-from-chord-shapes organization

> You may be curious as to how the many variations on a given harmonic formula are organized. Mainly by
> melodies (melody here refers to the highest pitch in the chords in almost all cases) starting from different forms (chord
> shapes). While playing through the examples in order, you will become aware of this. Remember, they are meant to be
> played in order.

**Significance (p. 26).** Explains organizing principle: variations indexed by top-voice melody from different chord shapes.

## V to I resolution rule

> One more thing: You should always try resolving the V to |. Some of the examples will give you ideas on how to
>
> do this, especially in the first few pages, but after this, you’re on your own.

**Significance (p. 26).** Categorical V→I resolution rule.

## Key-change freshness technique

> if anything in music ever sounds monotonous to you, changing
> the key can be like a breath of fresh air. Try it and see. Say you know a song in the key of G. Changing it to E or B
> will make a lot of difference.

**Significance (p. 27).** Rule for combating monotony via transposition.

## Fingering applies to filled noteheads

> Remember, the fingering applies
> io the , not the x (unless
> otherwise indicated).

**Significance (p. 29).** Notation rule: fingerings apply to filled noteheads not x noteheads.

## Continuity before value judgment

> play each
> one at least a couple of times with a reasonable amount of continuity and smoothness before making any value judg-
> ment about its worthiness.

**Significance (p. 26).** Methodological rule: continuity precedes evaluation.

## Physical difficulty as dexterity training

> you may run into physical difficulties at first which will prevent you
> from being able to hear the intended sound of some progressions. In these cases, continued practice of the examples
> which pose the probiems will help you in another way: the progressions then become your friends as far as developing
> the necessary manual dexterity and coordination which are essential to coping with this difficult instrument.

**Significance (p. 26).** Reframes physical struggle as dexterity training.

## Fingerboard exploration purpose

> Mainly to show just what can be done with a simple harmonic formula, and also for the purpose of exploring the
> entire fingerboard from many different angles.

**Significance (p. 26).** States the dual pedagogical purpose of the chapter's large example set.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
